heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Clinical guideline Reference number: CG184 Published: 03 September 2014 Last updated: 18 October 2019 Download guidance (PDF)

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: 1.1 The community pharmacist 1.2 Common elements of care 1.3 Referral guidance for endoscopy 1.4 Interventions for uninvestigated dyspepsia 1.5 Reviewing patient care 1.6 Interventions for GORD 1.7 Interventions for peptic ulcer disease 1.8 Interventions for functional dyspepsia 1.9 Helicobacter pylori testing and eradication 1.10 Laparoscopic fundoplication 1.11 Referral to a specialist service 1

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: People have the right to be involved in discussions and make informed decisions about their care, as described in NICE's information on making decisions about your care . Making decisions using NICE guidelines explains how we use words to show the strength (or certainty) of our recommendations, and has information about prescribing medicines (including off-label use), professional guidelines, stan

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Community pharmacists should offer initial and ongoing help for people with symptoms of dyspepsia. This includes advice about lifestyle changes, using over-the-counter medication, help with prescribed drugs and advice about when to consult a GP. [2004]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Community pharmacists should record adverse reactions to treatment and may participate in primary care medication review clinics. [2004]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Offer simple lifestyle advice, including advice on healthy eating, weight reduction and smoking cessation. [2004]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Advise people to avoid known precipitants they associate with their dyspepsia where possible. These include smoking, alcohol, coffee, chocolate, fatty foods and being overweight. Raising the head of the bed and having a main meal well before going to bed may help some people. [2004]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Provide people with access to educational materials to support the care they receive. [2004]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Recognise that psychological therapies, such as cognitive behavioural therapy and psychotherapy, may reduce dyspeptic symptoms in the short term in individual people. [2004, amended 2014]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Encourage people who need long-term management of dyspepsia symptoms to reduce their use of prescribed medication stepwise: by using the effective lowest dose, by trying 'as-needed' use when appropriate, and by returning to self-treatment with antacid and/or alginate therapy (unless there is an underlying condition or comedication that needs continuing treatment). [2004, amended 2014]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: For people presenting with dyspepsia together with significant acute gastrointestinal bleeding, refer them immediately (on the same day) to a specialist. Also see the NICE guideline on acute upper gastrointestinal bleeding in over 16s: management . [2004]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Review medications for possible causes of dyspepsia (for example, calcium antagonists, nitrates, theophyllines, bisphosphonates, corticosteroids and non-steroidal anti-inflammatory drugs [NSAIDs]). In people needing referral, suspend NSAID use. [2004]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Think about the possibility of cardiac or biliary disease as part of the differential diagnosis. [2004, amended 2014]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: If people have had a previous endoscopy and do not have any new alarm signs, consider continuing management according to previous endoscopic findings. [2004] For more information about alarm signs and when to refer people to specialists when they present with symptoms that could be caused by cancer, see the NICE guideline on suspected cancer: recognition and referral .

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Be aware that dyspepsia in unselected people in primary care is defined broadly to include people with recurrent epigastric pain, heartburn or acid regurgitation, with or without bloating, nausea or vomiting. Also see the section on common elements of care . [2004, amended 2014]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Leave a 2‑week washout period after proton pump inhibitor (PPI) use before testing for Helicobacter pylori (hereafter referred to as H pylori) with a breath test or a stool antigen test. [2004, amended 2014]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Offer empirical full-dose PPI therapy (see table 1 in appendix A ) for 4 weeks to people with dyspepsia . [2004]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Offer H pylori 'test and treat' to people with dyspepsia. [2004]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: If symptoms return after initial care strategies, step down PPI therapy to the lowest dose needed to control symptoms. Discuss using the treatment on an 'as-needed' basis with people to manage their own symptoms. [2004]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Offer H 2 receptor antagonist (H 2 RA) therapy if there is an inadequate response to a PPI. [2004, amended 2014]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Offer people who need long-term management of dyspepsia symptoms an annual review of their condition, and encourage them to try stepping down or stopping treatment (unless there is an underlying condition or comedication that needs continuing treatment). [2004, amended 2014]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Advise people that it may be appropriate for them to return to self-treatment with antacid and/or alginate therapy (either prescribed or purchased over-the-counter and taken as needed). [2004, amended 2014]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Manage uninvestigated 'reflux-like' symptoms as uninvestigated dyspepsia. [2004, amended 2014]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Offer people with GORD a full-dose PPI (see table 1 in appendix A ) for 4 or 8 weeks. [2004]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: If symptoms recur after initial treatment, offer a PPI at the lowest dose possible to control symptoms. [2004, amended 2014]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Discuss with people how they can manage their own symptoms by using the treatment when they need it. [2004]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Offer H 2 RA therapy if there is an inadequate response to a PPI. [2004, amended 2014]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: People who have had dilatation of an oesophageal stricture should remain on long-term full-dose PPI therapy (see table 1 in appendix A ). [2004]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Offer people a full-dose PPI (see table 2 in appendix A ) for 8 weeks to heal severe oesophagitis, taking into account the person's preference and clinical circumstances (for example, underlying health conditions and possible interactions with other drugs). [new 2014]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: If initial treatment for healing severe oesophagitis fails, consider a high dose of the initial PPI, switching to another full-dose PPI (see table 2) or switching to another high-dose PPI (see table 2 in appendix A ), taking into account the person's preference and clinical circumstances (for example, tolerability of the initial PPI, underlying health conditions and possible interactions with othe

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Offer a full-dose PPI (see table 2 in appendix A ) long-term as maintenance treatment for people with severe oesophagitis, taking into account the person's preference and clinical circumstances (for example, tolerability of the PPI, underlying health conditions and possible interactions with other drugs), and the acquisition cost of the PPI. [new 2014]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: If the person's severe oesophagitis fails to respond to maintenance treatment, carry out a clinical review. Consider switching to another PPI at full dose or high dose (see table 2 in appendix A ), taking into account the person's preference and clinical circumstances, and/or seeking specialist advice. [new 2014]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Do not routinely offer endoscopy to diagnose Barrett's oesophagus, but consider it if the person has GORD. Discuss the person's preferences and their individual risk factors (for example, long duration of symptoms, increased frequency of symptoms, previous oesophagitis, previous hiatus hernia, oesophageal stricture or oesophageal ulcers, or male gender). [new 2014]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Offer H pylori eradication therapy to people who have tested positive for H pylori and who have peptic ulcer disease. Also see the section on H pylori testing and eradication . [2004]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: For people using NSAIDs with diagnosed peptic ulcer, stop the use of NSAIDs where possible. Offer full-dose PPI (see table 1 in appendix A ) or H 2 RA therapy for 8 weeks and, if H pylori is present, subsequently offer eradication therapy. [2004]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Offer people with gastric ulcer and H pylori repeat endoscopy 6 to 8 weeks after beginning treatment, depending on the size of the lesion. [2004, amended 2014]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Offer people with peptic ulcer (gastric or duodenal) and H pylori retesting for H pylori 6 to 8 weeks after beginning treatment, depending on the size of the lesion. [2004, amended 2014]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Offer full-dose PPI (see table 1 in appendix A ) or H 2 RA therapy for 4 to 8 weeks to people who have tested negative for H pylori who are not taking NSAIDs. [2004]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: For people continuing to take NSAIDs after a peptic ulcer has healed, discuss the potential harm from NSAID treatment. Review the need for NSAID use regularly (at least every 6 months) and offer a trial of use on a limited, 'as-needed' basis. Consider reducing the dose, substituting an NSAID with paracetamol, or using an alternative analgesic or low-dose ibuprofen (1.2 g daily). [2004]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: In people at high risk (previous ulceration) and for whom NSAID continuation is necessary, consider a COX‑2 selective NSAID instead of a standard NSAID. In either case, prescribe with a PPI. [2004, amended 2014]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: In people with an unhealed ulcer, exclude non-adherence, malignancy, failure to detect H pylori, inadvertent NSAID use, other ulcer-inducing medication and rare causes such as Zollinger–Ellison syndrome or Crohn's disease. [2004]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: If symptoms recur after initial treatment, offer a PPI to be taken at the lowest dose possible to control symptoms. Discuss using the treatment on an 'as-needed' basis with people to manage their own symptoms. [2004, amended 2014]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Offer H 2 RA therapy if there is an inadequate response to a PPI. [2004]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Manage endoscopically determined functional dyspepsia using initial treatment for H pylori if present, followed by symptomatic management and periodic monitoring. [2004]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Offer eradication therapy to people testing positive for H pylori. [2004]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Do not routinely offer re-testing after eradication, although the information it provides may be valued by individual people. [2004]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: If H pylori has been excluded and symptoms persist, offer either a low-dose PPI (see table 1 in appendix A ) or an H 2 RA for 4 weeks. [2004, amended 2014]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: If symptoms continue or recur after initial treatment, offer a PPI or H 2 RA to be taken at the lowest dose possible to control symptoms. [2004, amended 2014]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Discuss using PPI treatment on an 'as-needed' basis with people to manage their own symptoms. [2004]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Avoid long-term, frequent dose, continuous antacid therapy (it only relieves symptoms in the short term rather than preventing them). [2004, amended 2014]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Test for H pylori using a carbon‑13 urea breath test or a stool antigen test, or laboratory-based serology where its performance has been locally validated. [2004, amended 2014]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Perform re-testing for H pylori using a carbon‑13 urea breath test. (There is currently insufficient evidence to recommend the stool antigen test as a test of eradication; this refers to evidence reviewed in 2004.) [2004]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Do not use office-based serological tests for H pylori because of their inadequate performance. [2004, amended 2014]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Offer people who test positive for H pylori a 7‑day, twice-daily course of treatment with: a PPI (see table 3 in appendix A ) and amoxicillin and either clarithromycin or metronidazole. Choose the treatment regimen with the lowest acquisition cost, and take into account previous exposure to clarithromycin or metronidazole. [new 2014]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Offer people who are allergic to penicillin a 7‑day, twice-daily course of treatment with: a PPI (see table 3 in appendix A ) and clarithromycin and metronidazole. [new 2014] For the assessment of allergy to beta-lactam antibiotics and referral to specialist care, see the NICE guideline on drug allergy .

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Offer people who are allergic to penicillin and who have had previous exposure to clarithromycin a 7‑day course of treatment with: a PPI (see table 3 in appendix A ) and bismuth and metronidazole and tetracycline. For the assessment of allergy to beta-lactam antibiotics and referral to specialist care, see the NICE guideline on drug allergy . [new 2014, amended 2019]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Discuss treatment adherence with the person and emphasise its importance. For more information about supporting adherence, see the NICE guideline on medicines adherence . [new 2014]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Offer people who still have symptoms after first-line eradication treatment a 7‑day, twice-daily course of treatment with: a PPI (see table 3 in appendix A ) and amoxicillin and either clarithromycin or metronidazole (whichever was not used first line). [new 2014]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Offer people who have had previous exposure to clarithromycin and metronidazole a 7‑day course of treatment with: a PPI (see table 3 in appendix A ) and amoxicillin and tetracycline (or, if a tetracycline cannot be used, levofloxacin). [new 2014, amended 2019] In September 2024, this was an off-label use of levofloxacin. See NICE's information on prescribing medicines .

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Offer people who are allergic to penicillin (and who have not had previous exposure to a fluoroquinolone antibiotic) a 7‑day, twice-daily course of treatment with: a PPI (see table 3 in appendix A ) and metronidazole and levofloxacin. For the assessment of allergy to beta-lactam antibiotics and referral to specialist care, see the NICE guideline on drug allergy . [new 2014, amended 2019] In Septem

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Offer people who are allergic to penicillin and who have had previous exposure to a fluoroquinolone antibiotic a 7‑day course of: a PPI (see table 3 in appendix A ) and bismuth and metronidazole and tetracycline. For the assessment of allergy to beta-lactam antibiotics and referral to specialist care, see the NICE guideline on drug allergy . [new 2014, amended 2019]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Seek advice from a gastroenterologist if eradication of H pylori is not successful with second-line treatment. [new 2014]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Consider laparoscopic fundoplication for people who have: a confirmed diagnosis of acid reflux and adequate symptom control with acid suppression therapy, but who do not wish to continue with this therapy long term a confirmed diagnosis of acid reflux and symptoms that are responding to a PPI, but who cannot tolerate acid suppression therapy. [new 2014]

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
paragraph_or_text_span: Consider referral to a specialist service for people: of any age with gastro-oesophageal symptoms that are non-responsive to treatment or unexplained (in the NICE guideline on suspected cancer: recognition and referral , 'unexplained' is defined as 'symptoms or signs that have not led to a diagnosis being made by the healthcare professional in primary care after initial assessment [including histo

heading_path: Gastro-oesophageal reflux disease and dyspepsia in adults: investigation and management
1.12 Surveillance for people with Barrett's oesophagus
paragraph_or_text_span: Consider surveillance to check progression to cancer for people who have a diagnosis of Barrett's oesophagus (confirmed by endoscopy and histopathology), taking into account: the presence of dysplasia (also see the NICE guideline on Barrett's oesophagus and stage 1 oesophageal adenocarcinoma ) the person's individual preference the person's risk factors (for example, male gender, older age and the
